medications.indication_rationale: Benign stimulant/stress-driven sinus tachycardia is treated by removing the cause (stop caffeine, rest, hydrate, reduce stress), not by drugs. No antiarrhythmic or rate-control agent is indicated, and none should be started remotely before an ECG has confirmed the rhythm.

A beta-blocker is not first-line for a transient stimulant-driven sinus tachycardia and must not be initiated via telehealth before an ECG and in-person assessment; symptomatic rate control is at most a later GP decision if symptoms persist after the cause is removed.

Prescribing a sedating anxiolytic remotely for acute palpitations is inappropriate: it can mask a deteriorating arrhythmia, carries dependence risk, and does not address the caffeine/stress cause. Reassurance and a guided breathing exercise are the correct non-pharmacological approach.

Adenosine is a hospital-only, monitored treatment for confirmed re-entrant SVT. It is contraindicated as a remote/telehealth intervention and would be dangerous if the rhythm is sinus tachycardia; it is listed here as an explicit error of commission.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop all caffeine and energy drinks immediately and discard the remaining supply.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Caffeine is the main exogenous driver; removing it allows the heart rate to settle as the stimulant washes out.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do a guided 10-minute slow, controlled breathing exercise now (e.g. box breathing).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Slow breathing engages the parasympathetic nervous system and directly counteracts the adrenaline surge, giving a measurable fall in heart rate and anxiety and restoring a sense of control.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Drink a couple of glasses of plain water and rehydrate.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Corrects any stimulant-related dehydration that can compound tachycardia.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest and prioritise sleep once symptoms settle.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Sleep deprivation from the work deadline sustains adrenergic drive; rest supports resolution.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep resting and note whether the heart rate settles over the next few hours; watch for the escalation warning signs.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Confirms the expected benign course and prompts escalation if it does not settle or red-flag features develop.

patient_education_points.point: Your heart is very likely reacting to a large amount of caffeine plus stress hormones, not failing — but because a fast heartbeat can occasionally be a different rhythm problem, it needs an ECG today to be sure.
Stopping the energy drinks, resting and slow breathing should let the rate settle as the caffeine clears your system.
Go for a same-day in-person check (your GP, an urgent care clinic or after-hours service) so they can do a heart tracing (ECG) and check your blood pressure.
Call 000 straight away if you get severe or crushing chest pain, actually faint or black out, or become short of breath at rest.
Arrange routine blood tests through your GP, including a thyroid test, to complete the picture.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person assessment for an ECG, then routine GP review in 48-72 hours
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: the ECG/rhythm and blood pressure, resolution of the tachycardia and symptoms, and results of TSH and routine bloods; reinforce caffeine and stress management
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: heart rate climbing higher, severe or crushing chest pain, actual fainting/blackout, or breathlessness at rest — escalate immediately (see safety-netting)

must_include_items: Recognise a sustained, unexplained tachycardia with chest pressure and presyncope as needing a same-day 12-lead ECG
Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP/urgent care/after-hours) rather than confirming or excluding a rhythm remotely
Identify and address the reversible drivers — stop all caffeine/energy drinks and manage acute stress
Offer a guided slow-breathing exercise and reassurance without prematurely closing the diagnosis remotely
Give clear safety-netting for chest pain, syncope and breathlessness with a 000 threshold
acceptable_alternatives: If the patient cannot readily access same-day in-person care, arranging the earliest possible ECG/urgent-care pathway while safety-netting is acceptable, provided the tachycardia is stable and no red flags are present.
Recommending routine TSH and FBC through the GP as part of the work-up.
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange a same-day ECG / in-person assessment
Failing to elicit the heavy caffeine intake as the likely driver
Failing to safety-net for chest pain, syncope or breathlessness
Falsely reassuring the patient it is 'just anxiety' with no plan to exclude an arrhythmia
errors_of_commission: Diagnosing SVT remotely and coaching vagal manoeuvres or recommending adenosine
Prescribing a beta-blocker or benzodiazepine via telehealth
Over-triaging a haemodynamically stable patient straight to a 000 ambulance
Ordering a reflexive troponin or urgent thyroid work-up without in-person assessment
Definitively labelling the rhythm as sinus tachycardia without an ECG
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires arranging a same-day in-person ECG/assessment AND safety-netting AND addressing the caffeine/stress driver, without prescribing rhythm-active drugs remotely or over-triaging to 000. Falsely reassuring remotely with no ECG plan, or attempting remote SVT treatment, is a failing response.
